focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

A yellow banner cut across the portal halfway through his third batch.

QUALITY REVIEW: BATCH 2-1147 SELECTED FOR CALIBRATION AUDIT.

Nick clicked it because yellow usually meant he'd fucked something up. The portal redrew itself into split screen, with his scores on the left and three expert reviewers on the right, their names replaced with strings of letters and numbers that made them look less like people than shipping labels.

The first image was one he'd rated twenty minutes earlier: a woman in an overcoat standing in a park with a paper cup in one hand and a wet bench behind her. At thumbnail size it looked plausible. Expanded, the problem was still where he'd left it. The sky was flat gray, the grass dull with overcast light, and under the woman's chin the model had painted a warm reflected glow from a wall that wasn't there.

He dragged the image larger anyway, checking himself. There was no bright pavement, no car catching sun, no low-angle source of any kind. He'd given lighting consistency a 2. The experts had given it 2, 2, and 2.

He clicked forward. Image nine was a hand wrapped around a ceramic mug. He'd rated anatomical accuracy a 1 because the wrist twisted farther than the radius and ulna would let a human arm go without breaking. Two experts had given it a 2. The third had given it a 1. A red line under the scores read SYSTEM REANALYSIS: EVALUATOR SCORE UPHELD.

By the time he reached image thirty-two he'd matched the experts on twenty-nine. On the three misses, he'd gone lower. Two were marked upheld. At the bottom of the summary page an alignment chart compared his scores to the floor mean. His bar sat above the gray average by enough that he refreshed once to make sure the page wasn't stuck on someone else's account.

Then a note expan...
open_hooks_to_carry: The physical reality of the labeling floor — fluorescent lights, beige carpet, rows of monitors, the murmur of rating
Introduction of all four key coworkers: Dani, Tommy, Bao, Priya — each must register as a distinct person, not a type
The evaluation rubric and its categories: prompt adherence, anatomical accuracy, lighting consistency, compositional balance, stylistic coherence
Nick's realization that his coworkers are mostly artists
prev_tail_excerpt:
...screen, he let it ring once before he answered.

"Nick?" Priya said. "It's Priya Acharya from Canopy."

"Yeah."

"We'd like to make you an offer for the evaluator role. Recruiting just sent the paperwork. It's contingent on the standard background check and your I-9 documents, but assuming those clear, orientation is Monday at eight with Lisa. Pay is twenty an hour. Can you confirm?"

Nick looked at the wall where his canvases were stacked face-in, all that dead weight of stretched linen and failed rent. "Yes," he said. "Yeah. I can do Monday."

"Good. Check your spam if the email doesn't show up. There are a lot of forms."

The packet hit his inbox before she hung up: offer letter, tax forms, confidentiality agreement, badge photo instructions, parking map, a PDF called Data Integrity Standards. He opened that one before the tax forms. It listed falsified ratings, misuse of internal tools, deliberate degradation of label quality, and immediate termination for any of it. He read the phrase deliberate degradation twice. The company already had a bureaucratic name for the thing he wanted to do. By the second read it sounded almost stupid enough to work.

He took the badge photo against the kitchen wall because it was the only blank surface in the apartment. The upload tool rejected the first image for shadows and the second for low resolution. On the third try his face came back to him in a little square with a green check mark under it, approved for printing.

He texted his two references after midnight: please answer if San Leandro calls, please say I can learn fast, please don't ask why yet.

He had the weekend to dig up his Social Security card from the drawer with old gallery postcards and dead paint pens, sign seven PDFs, and get himself through the door on Monday.
state_deltas_required: Nick enters a world he expected to despise and finds it populated by people like him
Saboteur enters the building and discovers the human landscape he will have to betray
